args_redacted.query: zero trust security model principles never trust always verify
defense in depth layered security strategy explained

args_redacted.raw:
{"path": "workspace/security_comparison.txt", "content": "================================================================================
  Zero Trust Security vs. Defense in Depth: A Comparative Analysis
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
    Zero Trust is a security model built on the principle of "never trust,
    always verify." It assumes that threats can exist both outside and inside
    the network perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or application
    should be trusted by default — even if they are already inside the
    network. Every access request must be authenticated, authorized, and
    continuously validated before granting access to resources.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
    Defense in Depth is a layered security strategy that employs multiple,
    overlapping defensive controls to protect information assets. The idea
    is that if one layer fails, the next layer will catch the threat. It
    draws inspiration from military strategy, where multiple lines of
    defense slow and ultimately stop an attacker. Layers span physical
    security, network security, endpoint security, application security,
    data security, and user awareness.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES                    | DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES
-----------------------------------------|---------------------------------------
Never trust, always verify               | No single point of failure
Least-privilege access                   | Layered controls (defense in layers)
Micro-segmentation                       | Redundancy and overlapping safeguards
Assume breach                            | Deter, detect, delay, respond
Continuous monitoring & validation       | Physical, technical, administrative
Explicit verification (authN + authZ)    | Perimeter, network, host, app, data
Dynamic policy enforcement               | Security across all domains


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A. PHILOSOPHY
    Zero Trust: Skeptical by default. Trust is a vulnerability. Every
    interaction is treated as potentially hostile until proven otherwise.

    Defense in Depth: Pragmatic layering. Recognizes that any single control
    can fail, so multiple controls are deployed to compensate. Trust is
    managed through layers rather than eliminated.

B. APPROACH TO THE NETWORK PERIMETER
    Zero Trust: The traditional network perimeter is considered irrelevant.
    The focus shifts to protecting individual resources (data, applications,
    services) regardless of where the request originates. Micro-segmentation
    breaks the network into tiny zones, each requiring separate access.

    Defense in Depth: The perimeter is still a meaningful concept — it is
    simply one layer among many. Firewalls, IDS/IPS, and DMZs form the outer
    layers, with additional controls stacked behind them.

C. ACCESS CONTROL
    Zero Trust: Access is granted per session, per resource, based on
    dynamic risk evaluation (user identity, device health, location,
    behavior patterns). Continuous re-verification is required.

    Defense in Depth: Access control is typically layered — network-level
    (firewall rules), system-level (OS authentication), application-level
    (app login), and data-level (encryption, rights management). Each layer
    operates somewhat independently.

D. ASSUMPTION OF COMPROMISE
    Zero Trust: Explicitly assumes breach has already occurred or will
    occur. Architectures are designed to limit blast radius and lateral
    movement from the start.

    Defense in Depth: Acknowledges that breaches can happen but focuses
    more on preventing them through cumulative layers. Breach assumption
    is implicit rather than foundational.

E. IMPLEMENTATION COMPLEXITY
    Zero Trust: Often requires significant architectural transformation —
    identity-aware proxies, software-defined perimeters, micro-segmentation,
    and continuous monitoring infrastructure. Can be disruptive to legacy
    environments.

    Defense in Depth: Can be implemented incrementally. Organizations can
    add layers over time (e.g., add endpoint protection on top of existing
    firewalls). More compatible with legacy systems and gradual adoption.


4. STRENGTHS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST STRENGTHS:
    • Strong protection against lateral movement after a breach
    • Granular, context-aware access control
    • Well-suited for cloud-native, remote-work, and hybrid environments
    • Reduces the attack surface by eliminating implicit trust
    • Aligns with modern identity-centric security paradigms
    • Effective against insider threats

DEFENSE IN DEPTH STRENGTHS:
    • Proven, time-tested approach with decades of real-world validation
    • No single point of failure — redundancy increases resilience
    • Flexible and adaptable

{"content": "================================================================================
  Zero Trust Security vs. Defense in Depth: A Comparative Analysis
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
    Zero Trust is a security model built on the principle of "never trust,
    always verify." It assumes that threats can exist both outside and inside
    the network perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or application
    should be trusted by default — even if they are already inside the
    network. Every access request must be authenticated, authorized, and
    continuously validated before granting access to resources.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
    Defense in Depth is a layered security strategy that employs multiple,
    overlapping defensive controls to protect information assets. The idea
    is that if one layer fails, the next layer will catch the threat. It
    draws inspiration from military strategy, where multiple lines of
    defense slow and ultimately stop an attacker. Layers span physical
    security, network security, endpoint security, application security,
    data security, and user awareness.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES                    | DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES
-----------------------------------------|---------------------------------------
Never trust, always verify               | No single point of failure
Least-privilege access                   | Layered controls (defense in layers)
Micro-segmentation                       | Redundancy and overlapping safeguards
Assume breach                            | Deter, detect, delay, respond
Continuous monitoring & validation       | Physical, technical, administrative
Explicit verification (authN + authZ)    | Perimeter, network, host, app, data
Dynamic policy enforcement               | Security across all domains


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A. PHILOSOPHY
    Zero Trust: Skeptical by default. Trust is a vulnerability. Every
    interaction is treated as potentially hostile until proven otherwise.

    Defense in Depth: Pragmatic layering. Recognizes that any single control
    can fail, so multiple controls are deployed to compensate. Trust is
    managed through layers rather than eliminated.

B. APPROACH TO THE NETWORK PERIMETER
    Zero Trust: The traditional network perimeter is considered irrelevant.
    The focus shifts to protecting individual resources (data, applications,
    services) regardless of where the request originates. Micro-segmentation
    breaks the network into tiny zones, each requiring separate access.

    Defense in Depth: The perimeter is still a meaningful concept — it is
    simply one layer among many. Firewalls, IDS/IPS, and DMZs form the outer
    layers, with additional controls stacked behind them.

C. ACCESS CONTROL
    Zero Trust: Access is granted per session, per resource, based on
    dynamic risk evaluation (user identity, device health, location,
    behavior patterns). Continuous re-verification is required.

    Defense in Depth: Access control is typically layered — network-level
    (firewall rules), system-level (OS authentication), application-level
    (app login), and data-level (encryption, rights management). Each layer
    operates somewhat independently.

D. ASSUMPTION OF COMPROMISE
    Zero Trust: Explicitly assumes breach has already occurred or will
    occur. Architectures are designed to limit blast radius and lateral
    movement from the start.

    Defense in Depth: Acknowledges that breaches can happen but focuses
    more on preventing them through cumulative layers. Breach assumption
    is implicit rather than foundational.

E. IMPLEMENTATION COMPLEXITY
    Zero Trust: Often requires significant architectural transformation —
    identity-aware proxies, software-defined perimeters, micro-segmentation,
    and continuous monitoring infrastructure. Can be disruptive to legacy
    environments.

    Defense in Depth: Can be implemented incrementally. Organizations can
    add layers over time (e.g., add endpoint protection on top of existing
    firewalls). More compatible with legacy systems and gradual adoption.


4. STRENGTHS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST STRENGTHS:
    • Strong protection against lateral movement after a breach
    • Granular, context-aware access control
    • Well-suited for cloud-native, remote-work, and hybrid environments
    • Reduces the attack surface by eliminating implicit trust
    • Aligns with modern identity-centric security paradigms
    • Effective against insider threats

DEFENSE IN DEPTH STRENGTHS:
    • Proven, time-tested approach with decades of real-world validation
    • No single point of failure — redundancy increases resilience
    • Flexible and adaptable to diverse environments
    • Can be implemented incrementally without wholesale architectural change
    • Covers physical, technical, and human/administrative domains
    • Easier to align with compliance frameworks (PCI DSS, HIPAA, etc.)


5. WEA

{"content": "================================================================================
  Zero Trust Security vs. Defense in Depth: A Comparative Analysis
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
    Zero Trust is a security model built on the principle of "never trust,
    always verify." It assumes that threats can exist both outside and inside
    the network perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or application
    should be trusted by default — even if they are already inside the
    network. Every access request must be authenticated, authorized, and
    continuously validated before granting access to resources.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
    Defense in Depth is a layered security strategy that employs multiple,
    overlapping defensive controls to protect information assets. The idea
    is that if one layer fails, the next layer will catch the threat. It
    draws inspiration from military strategy, where multiple lines of
    defense slow and ultimately stop an attacker. Layers span physical
    security, network security, endpoint security, application security,
    data security, and user awareness.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES                    | DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES
-----------------------------------------|---------------------------------------
Never trust, always verify               | No single point of failure
Least-privilege access                   | Layered controls (defense in layers)
Micro-segmentation                       | Redundancy and overlapping safeguards
Assume breach                            | Deter, detect, delay, respond
Continuous monitoring & validation       | Physical, technical, administrative
Explicit verification (authN + authZ)    | Perimeter, network, host, app, data
Dynamic policy enforcement               | Security across all domains


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A. PHILOSOPHY
    Zero Trust: Skeptical by default. Trust is a vulnerability. Every
    interaction is treated as potentially hostile until proven otherwise.

    Defense in Depth: Pragmatic layering. Recognizes that any single control
    can fail, so multiple controls are deployed to compensate. Trust is
    managed through layers rather than eliminated.

B. APPROACH TO THE NETWORK PERIMETER
    Zero Trust: The traditional network perimeter is considered irrelevant.
    The focus shifts to protecting individual resources (data, applications,
    services) regardless of where the request originates. Micro-segmentation
    breaks the network into tiny zones, each requiring separate access.

    Defense in Depth: The perimeter is still a meaningful concept — it is
    simply one layer among many. Firewalls, IDS/IPS, and DMZs form the outer
    layers, with additional controls stacked behind them.

C. ACCESS CONTROL
    Zero Trust: Access is granted per session, per resource, based on
    dynamic risk evaluation (user identity, device health, location,
    behavior patterns). Continuous re-verification is required.

    Defense in Depth: Access control is typically layered — network-level
    (firewall rules), system-level (OS authentication), application-level
    (app login), and data-level (encryption, rights management). Each layer
    operates somewhat independently.

D. ASSUMPTION OF COMPROMISE
    Zero Trust: Explicitly assumes breach has already occurred or will
    occur. Architectures are designed to limit blast radius and lateral
    movement from the start.

    Defense in Depth: Acknowledges that breaches can happen but focuses
    more on preventing them through cumulative layers. Breach assumption
    is implicit rather than foundational.

E. IMPLEMENTATION COMPLEXITY
    Zero Trust: Often requires significant architectural transformation —
    identity-aware proxies, software-defined perimeters, micro-segmentation,
    and continuous monitoring infrastructure. Can be disruptive to legacy
    environments.

    Defense in Depth: Can be implemented incrementally. Organizations can
    add layers over time (e.g., add endpoint protection on top of existing
    firewalls). More compatible with legacy systems and gradual adoption.


4. STRENGTHS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST STRENGTHS:
    • Strong protection against lateral movement after a breach
    • Granular, context-aware access control
    • Well-suited for cloud-native, remote-work, and hybrid environments
    • Reduces the attack surface by eliminating implicit trust
    • Aligns with modern identity-centric security paradigms
    • Effective against insider threats

DEFENSE IN DEPTH STRENGTHS:
    • Proven, time-tested approach with decades of real-world validation
    • No single point of failure — redundancy increases resilience
    • Flexible and adaptable to diverse environments
    • Can be implemented incrementally without wholesale architectural change
    • Covers physical, technical, and human/administ
